mappings.rationale: Provides the broad framework for understanding human learning, memory, and cognitive processes.
Directly covers memory, retention, and forgetting of learned habits and content (section E), including amnesia and memory enhancement.
Directly covers theories of learning, major issues in learning theory, and transfer of training (sections A and B).
Directly covers the higher thought processes (section G), including reasoning and the psychology of thinking.
Directly covers the psychology of higher thought processes, concept formation, and ideation (sections D, G).
Covers the neural basis of learning, memory, and higher cognitive processes including conceptual learning and thought.
Covers brain mechanisms underlying learning, memory formation, and the neural substrates of thought and reasoning.
Relevant to the higher thought processes and the role of conscious awareness in learning and concept formation.
Directly relevant to perceptual learning (section C), the process by which perceptual skills develop through experience.
Directly relevant to the role of language in the higher thought processes (section G.2) and the concept of meaning (section G.3).
Relevant to the role of language in higher thought processes and how language structures influence concept formation and meaning.
Directly covers the intersection of language and cognition, relevant to the role of language in higher thought processes and meaning (sections G.2, G.3).
Covers learning and cognitive development in children, relevant to conceptual learning, concept formation, and psychomotor learning.
Pavlov's conditioning research is foundational to modern learning theories (section A.1) and major issues in learning theory.
Covers the applied dimension of learning theories, including transfer of training (section A.3) and pedagogical approaches.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: Directly covers meaning (section G.3), the philosophical and linguistic dimensions of how meaning is constructed through thought and language.
Relevant to abnormalities of memory including amnesia, confabulation, and the breakdown of learned habits and cognitive function (section F).
A specific learning difficulty relevant to perceptual and conceptual learning processes and their disruption.
Relevant to higher thought processes (section G) and the generation of novel concepts and ideas beyond standard reasoning.
Machine learning provides comparative models for understanding human learning theories and concept formation processes.
Relevant to higher thought processes (section G) and the cognitive processes of ideation and concept formation.
Freudian theory addresses abnormalities of memory including repression, and the role of unconscious processes in learning and forgetting.
Relevant to psychomotor learning (section B) and the development of bodily skills through practice and training.
